Framework · free to use

A 90-day AI implementation roadmap that actually ships.

Most AI implementation roadmaps are vague. They list "adopt AI" as a milestone. This is a concrete 90-day template — week by week, owner by owner, deliverable by deliverable — built from the rollouts we have run at B2B companies between $5M and $50M.

By Bill Colbert · Founder, Treetop Growth Strategy
Published May 2026 · More from the library
How to use this

Read this once, then customize

This template is opinionated. It assumes a B2B team of 10–50 people, a CEO or COO sponsor, and at least one operations person who can own the work. If you are bigger or more regulated, the timeline stretches; if you are smaller and faster, it compresses.

Three rules before you start. (1) Pick one revenue function and one operations function — do not try to roll AI out everywhere. (2) Name a single human owner per workflow you change — no committees. (3) Define what "shipped" looks like for each phase before you start that phase.

A 90-day roadmap fails the same way every time: leaders treat it as a slide deck instead of a calendar. Put every milestone in a real calendar with a named owner and a checkpoint date. Half of execution is just making the schedule visible.

Phase 1: Days 1-30

Foundations, audit, and one quick win

Week 1 — Alignment

  • CEO statement of intent — one paragraph: what is the goal, who owns it, what is the budget envelope.
  • Name the AI lead — one human, 8-12 hrs/week minimum. Not a committee.
  • Inventory current AI usage — who is already using what (ChatGPT, Copilot, Claude, etc.), paid or shadow.

Week 2 — Audit

  • Map the 10 most time-consuming repeatable workflows across sales, marketing, ops, and admin.
  • Rank them by leverage: time saved × frequency × confidence in AI fit.
  • Pick the top 3. The rest go on the backlog.

Week 3 — Tool selection & access

  • Decide on the LLM platform. For most teams: Claude Team for general business use; ChatGPT Team if your stack is already Microsoft-heavy.
  • Provision seats for everyone in the affected workflows, not just the AI lead.
  • Confirm data handling: which models train on your data (avoid them for client work), which do not.

Week 4 — One quick win shipped

  • Pick the smallest of your top 3 workflows. Build the Project, write the system prompt, train the one or two people who will use it daily.
  • Ship it for real. Not a demo — actual production use.
  • Phase 1 deliverable: one workflow visibly working, with measurable time saved.
Phase 2: Days 31-60

Ship the remaining two priority workflows

Week 5-6 — Workflow #2

  • Same loop: define workflow, build Project, write system prompt, train users, ship.
  • Add a weekly review: 30 minutes with the AI lead and the workflow owner to look at real outputs and refine.

Week 7-8 — Workflow #3

  • By now the team has a pattern. Workflow #3 should ship faster than #1 did.
  • Document what you have learned about your team — what works, what fails, what people resist.

If you have not shipped any of your top 3 workflows by end of week 8, stop adding workflows. Diagnose why the first one stalled. Usually the answer is no named owner or no time carved out — both fixable, neither solved by adding scope.

Phase 3: Days 61-90

Scale, train, govern

Week 9-10 — Adjacent workflows

  • Each shipped workflow has neighbors. The proposal workflow has a follow-up email workflow. The content workflow has a distribution workflow. Pick 2-3 neighbors and ship them.

Week 11 — Training the rest of the team

  • Hold a 90-minute internal training where the workflow owners demo what they built.
  • Publish a one-page AI Usage Policy: what is OK to put into AI tools, what is not, who to ask.

Week 12 — Review & next quarter

  • Review measured impact: hours saved, cycle time changes, output volume changes.
  • Set Q2 priorities: which 3 new workflows ship next, with which owners.
  • Phase 3 deliverable: 5-7 workflows in production, a published policy, a named owner per workflow, and a Q2 backlog.
The owner checklist

Print this and tape it to the AI lead's wall

  • Every workflow has a named human owner — not a committee, not a function.
  • Every workflow has a measurable outcome defined before work starts.
  • Every workflow has a weekly checkpoint with the AI lead in the first month.
  • No workflow is launched without the people who will use it being trained on it.
  • No workflow is left running without someone reviewing outputs monthly.
  • Every workflow has documented prompts, projects, and templates stored in a shared location.
What this template does not cover

Where you will need help

Workflows that touch regulated data (HIPAA, SOC 2, financial advice) require an additional 30-45 days of compliance work. Add that to the front of the timeline if you are in those industries.

Enterprise rollouts (200+ people) need a different model — federated by function with a small central enablement team. This template is for teams under 100.

If you have never deployed software internally before, the change-management work is bigger than the AI work. Plan for that explicitly.
